ProjectDescription: The NPO is a nonprofit organization supporting people in need by providing basic necessities with dignity and respect. In addition to welfare, it empowers children and youth in Israel’s social and geographic periphery. Operating 34 facilities nationwide—including community kitchens, shelters, youth centers, and a daycare—it provides around 2,000 hot meals daily. Its “City Harvest” initiative rescues surplus food from bakeries, suppliers, restaurants, hotels, corporate dining halls, and IDF bases. A digital platform will allow businesses to report donations with all relevant details, enabling real-time notifications, approval, and smart route planning to increase efficiency, expand donors, and rescue more food.

Lists.Name: Sprint 8
Lists.Description:
This is the final stabilization push. Your mission is to switch from feature building to a collaborative "Bug Hunting" phase alongside the entire team to ensure the platform is production-ready. 
 
 You are moving from the "feature" phase to the "quality" phase. This sprint is entirely focused on identifying glitches and ensuring the user experience is flawless before the final handoff. Since everyone is doing QA, you will monitor the Meeting Room for every "Bugs case" logged and address them in your dedicated branch. 
 
 Success means a pixel-perfect, error-free frontend that has been fully merged into the main repository.

Cards.Name: Infrastructure Audit & High-Level Data Modeling
Cards.Description:
Welcome to the team! 
 
 This week is your "Technical Landing": a low-pressure space to explore the project's foundations. 
 
 Your objective is to partner with the PM to translate their business journey into a technical blueprint. 
 
 You will verify the environment and create the "Big Picture" database model that will serve as our project's technical map.
ChecklistItems: [ ] Team Kickoff: Participate in the "Project Kickoff" to meet your teammates and understand the project’s high-level mission.
[ ] Local Repository Setup: Clone the provided GitHub repository to your local developer environment to begin your technical exploration.
[ ] Sprint Branch Initialization (Mentor Panel): Use the mentor buttons panel to create your official branch for the current sprint.
[ ] Local Sync Verification (Local Environment): From your local environment, push a "Health Check" commit to your branch to confirm your local IDE is perfectly synced with the repository.

[ ] GitHub Cycle Practice (Mentor Panel): Return to the mentor buttons panel to create a Pull Request (PR) and merge your branch into main. Use this to gain a hands-on understanding of the project's GitHub cycle.
[ ] Infrastructure Audit: Connect to the Postgres DB and perform a "Stress Test" (e.g., creating and deleting a dummy table) to ensure the environment is stable for future data.

[ ] Collaborative Journey & Persona Review: Sit down with the PM to review their Global Journey Diagram and review the UI/UX Designer's new User Personas. Use this session to brainstorm the core entities and relationships (e.g., Donors, Coordinators, Drivers) needed to support the entire system.
[ ] High-Level Schema Diagram: Based on your review with the PM, create a visual ERD (Entity Relationship Diagram) showing the high-level data architecture.
[ ] Blueprint Centralization: Save your schema diagram in the Meeting Room so the team can reference our "Data Source of Truth." (Your Mentor can guide you on the process).

Cards.Name: Frontend Orientation & Environmental Foundation
Cards.Description:
Welcome to the front line! 
 
 We are thrilled to have you on board. 
 
 This first week is your "Technical Landing" - a low-pressure space to explore your new digital playground. 
 
 While the PM and UI/UX teams define the project's soul, your objective is to ensure your development environment is perfectly tuned and to collaborate on the project's global structure. 
 
 You are the team's bridge between design and code.
ChecklistItems: [ ] Team Kickoff: Participate in the "Sprint 1 Kickoff" to meet the team and align on our high-level mission.
[ ] Local Repository Setup: Clone the provided GitHub repository to your local developer environment to begin your technical setup.
[ ] Sprint Branch Initialization (Mentor Panel): Use the mentor buttons panel to create your official branch for the current sprint.

[ ] Local Sync Verification (Local Environment): From your local environment, push a "Health Check" commit to your branch to confirm your local IDE and build tools are perfectly synced with the repository.

[ ] GitHub Cycle Practice (Mentor Panel): Return to the mentor buttons panel to create a Pull Request (PR) and merge your branch into main. Use this to gain a hands-on understanding of the project's GitHub cycle.
[ ] Tech Stack Audit: Explore the project's frontend boilerplate (React/Next.js/etc.) and verify that all necessary dependencies and linting rules are correctly installed.
[ ] Collaborative Journey Review: Sit down with the PM and the Backend Developer to review the Global Journey Diagram. Use this to identify potential UI complexities or navigation challenges early.

[ ] Global Styling Foundation: Based on the UI/UX Designer's initial research, begin initializing the project's "Foundations"—set up the global CSS variables for the color palette, typography scales, and spacing units.

Cards.Name: Ecosystem & CRM Foundation
Cards.Description:
Welcome to the team! 
 
 We are so excited to have you as our "Market Intelligence" lead. 
 
 This first week is a low-pressure opportunity for you to learn about the industry we are entering. 
 
 Your mission is to map out the "playing field" - the ecosystem of food distribution - and identify who the key players are. 
 
 Think of the AI Mentor as your research consultant who will hold your hand while you learn our internal CRM module. 
 
 This week is all about discovery and building a solid knowledge base for the team.
ChecklistItems: [ ] Team Kickoff: Join the "Sprint 1 Kickoff" to meet the team and understand our shared starting point.
[ ] Anchor Alignment: Start an introductory conversation with the AI Customer to understand the business world they operate in every day.
[ ] CRM & Research Setup: Ask the AI Mentor for a step-by-step walkthrough of the CRM module and for professional guidance on creating a "Stakeholder Map."
[ ] Industry Discovery: Conduct "Desk Research" on the food distribution world to understand how it works (e.g., how food gets from a donor to a person in need).
[ ] Competitive Analysis: Review 2-3 existing global solutions and summarize how they approach this industry.
[ ] Stakeholder Mapping: Identify the key groups involved (donors, beneficiaries, and regulators) and input these entities into your CRM screen.
[ ] NSM Prep: Get familiar with the term NSM (Next Sprint Meeting); you’ll share your ecosystem findings here to help the team stay grounded in the industry's reality.

Cards.Name: Vision, Discovery & The PRD Foundation
Cards.Description:
Welcome to the helm! 
 
 Your mission this week is to transform initial ambiguity into a solid strategic roadmap. 
 
 You are the Strategic Detective, digging deep into the "Why" and "Who" of this project. 
 
 The Big Picture: While we are establishing the foundations now, keep in mind that the actual construction of our first module begins in Sprint 3. 
 
 This week is about building the trust and the blueprint: the Project PRD - required to make that build successful.
ChecklistItems: [ ] Project Kickoff: Lead the official "Project Kickoff" in the meeting room. Set the vision, align the team on the mission, and facilitate introductions.
[ ] Kickoff Synthesis: Publish a concise meeting summary and a list of immediate action items in the Team Chat to ensure Day 1 alignment.
[ ] Relationship Building: Initiate introductory conversations with each teammate (BE, FE, UI/UX, BizDev) to establish a personal connection and a supportive team culture.
[ ] Team Connection Recap: Post a high-level summary of the team’s collective strengths and support needs in the Team Chat to reinforce the collaborative foundation.
[ ] PRD Initialization: Initialize the Product Requirements Document (PRD). This is the central home for the project’s DNA. Use it to document every strategic decision you make this week.

[ ] Problem Validation: Interview the AI Customer to confirm reality vs. assumptions regarding their current workflow. Document their "Pain Points" and "Success Criteria" in the Discovery section of your PRD.
[ ] Customer Insight Broadcast: Share the key takeaways and prioritized action items from the customer interview in the Team Chat to ground the team in the user's reality.
[ ] North Star & KPIs: Define the project's North Star Metric and 3–5 supporting KPIs. Add these to the PRD to serve as the decision filter for the entire journey.
[ ] Actor Alignment: Identify the main actors (e.g., Donors, Volunteers, Admins). Map out their primary responsibilities and "Needs" within the User Persona section of the PRD.
[ ] Journey Mapping & Centralization: Create a visual diagram of the end-to-end food collection journey. Integrate this diagram directly into the PRD so it serves as the project’s visual anchor.
[ ] PRD Accessibility: Save the PRD in the Squad Room. Ensure it is available to everyone and clearly marked as the project's "Source of Truth."
[ ] Team Journey Demonstration: Set a meeting with the entire team to demonstrate the Journey Map and walk through the Draft PRD. Gather feedback and ensure everyone sees the "Big Picture."
[ ] Vision Alignment Summary: Publish the final meeting summary and a consolidated list of feedback-driven action items in the Team Chat to officially close the loop on Sprint 1.

Cards.Name: Ecosystem Discovery & Research Foundation
Cards.Description:
Welcome to the team! 
 
 As our lead designer, you are the architect of the user's experience. 
 
 This first week is your "Discovery" phase: a low-pressure space to understand the industry and define who we are building for. 
 
 Before diving into the visuals, you will coordinate your research roadmap with the Product Manager. 
 
 Your goal is to gather the competitive intelligence and persona insights needed to shape a high-impact design strategy in the weeks ahead.
ChecklistItems: [ ] Project Kickoff: Participate in the Project Kickoff meeting to align on the mission and meet your new teammates.
[ ] Product Manager Alignment: Meet with the Product Manager to plan your specific research tasks and milestones for the remainder of the sprint.
[ ] Figma Setup: Initialize your Figma project and link it to the Meeting Room. Ask the AI Mentor if you need instructions on how to sync the files.

[ ] Market Research: Analyze existing solutions in related fields (e.g., volunteer management, logistics, or food distribution). Note the pros and cons of each and upload a summary to the Meeting Room.
[ ] Visual Inspiration Board: Create a Mood Board or "Inspiration Table" in Figma. Focus on UI patterns, color psychology, and layouts that align with the project goals.
[ ] User Personas: Define initial User Personas (e.g., donor, coordinator, driver). Document their specific needs, motivations, and potential pain points. Add persona designs to the project's Figma.
[ ] User Journey Insights: Review the Global User Journey created by the Product Manager. Flag any specific areas or flows that require extra UX attention.

Cards.Name: Data Architecture & Technical Readiness
Cards.Description:
This week is about definition over execution. 
 
 Your goal is to move from the abstract "Big Picture" to a precise technical blueprint for the Donor Submission Experience. 
 
 You are the architect of the data flow. 
 
 By the end of this week, you will have established the plan for how the Frontend and Backend communicate. 
 
 You aren't building the final logic yet; you are preparing the "manual" so that the team can build with total confidence in Sprint 3.
ChecklistItems: Environment Launch: Launch the sprint and create your Sprint 2 branch via the mentor buttons panel to begin your technical exploration.

Tooling and Library Audit: Review your current backend stack and libraries (e.g., validation libraries, database drivers). Ensure everything is optimized and ready for the specific data-entry and form-handling tasks coming next week.

Frontend Data Sync (General Handshake): Meet with the Frontend Developer to agree on the general "Blueprint" for how data will move between the UI and the server (e.g., Auth patterns, error handling, and endpoint structure).

Requirements Sync (Tables and Fields): Once the PM has finalized the User Story, meet to identify the specific Tables and Fields required to support the Donor Form. Map out exactly what data points need to be stored (e.g., quantities and timestamps).

Database Schema Blueprint: Update the Database Schema diagram based on the PM's finalized fields. This acts as the "Contract" for the project's data structure and serves as the team's single source of truth.
Mock Data Provision: Generate a set of "dummy" Donation data. The goal is to give the Frontend Developer realistic data to work with in Sprint 3 before you have built the actual database logic.
Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Participate in the NSM to present your technical blueprint. Confirm that your architecture can support the validation rules required by the customer.

Blueprint Delivery (PR and Merge): Commit your final schema designs and mock data files to your branch. Create a Pull Request (PR) and merge it into main via the mentor buttons panel to lock in the technical blueprint for the build.

Cards.Name: UI Architecture & Component Scaffolding
Cards.Description:
This week, you are the "Architect" of the interface. 
 
 Before we start coding the actual logic for the Donor Submission Experience in Sprint 3, you will build the reusable "Atoms" that make development fast and consistent. 
 
 You are building the "lego pieces" and the "hallways" of the application. 
 
 By the end of the week, the team should be able to click through the basic navigation of the flow, even if the forms don't "save" data yet. 
 
 This is where we build the skeleton so that next week, we can add the muscle.
ChecklistItems: Environment Launch: Launch the sprint and create your Sprint 2 branch via the mentor buttons panel to begin your technical exploration.
Component Library Initialization: Set up a dedicated workspace for your reusable UI components (e.g., a /components library folder or a Storybook setup).

Atomic Component Build: Build the foundational, "logic-less" components required for the Donor Form: Buttons, Input Fields (Text/Select), Loaders, and Success Modals. Apply the Visual Language (Colors and Typography) established by the Designer this week.

Frontend Data Sync: Meet with the Backend Developer to agree on the "Data Blueprint." Confirm the specific "Request" (what the form sends) and "Response" (what the server says back) so that your components are ready for real data.

App Navigation "Hallways": Set up the empty page containers and the URL paths for the Donor flow (e.g., /submit for the form and /success for the confirmation). This ensures the structural journey is ready for the logic in Sprint 3.
UI/UX Handshake: Review the Designer’s final design. Ensure general synchronization regarding technical feasibility and confirm that the layout aligns with the UI components you are preparing.
Milestone: Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Participate in the NSM to present your component library and technical readiness. Show the team the "UI Atoms" and the clickable "Hallways" you’ve built.

UI Delivery (PR & Merge): Commit your foundational components and navigation routes to your branch. Create a Pull Request (PR) and merge it into main via the mentor buttons panel. This locks in the UI architecture before the build phase begins.

Cards.Name: Market Intelligence & Donor Validation
Cards.Description:
This week, you serve as the project's Strategic Pulse. 
 
 Your mission is to maintain a dual-stream focus: Internal Alignment to deeply understand the product's technical evolution, and External Validation to ground that progress in market reality. 
 
 You are responsible for ensuring that the Donor Submission Experience is not just a technical achievement, but a market-ready solution. 
 
 You will bridge the gap between the development team's potential constraints and the actual operational habits of restaurants, markets, and hotels.
ChecklistItems:
Customer Sync: At the start of the week, consult with the AI Customer to identify "unknowns" regarding Donor data. 
 Focus: Clarify operational details that will affect the build (e.g., "Do donors usually know the exact weight of surplus food, or do they think in terms of 'trays' or 'bags'?").

Internal Product Alignment: Once the PM and UI/UX Designer have drafted the initial User Story and design, meet to conduct a deep-dive. 
 Goal: Ensure you can articulate exactly how the intake logic and validations are intended to work before you head into the field.

Field Stakeholder Outreach: Conduct targeted interviews with 2-3 potential donors (e.g., local restaurant managers, bakery owners, or fruit/vegetable market vendors). 
 Validation: Present the high-level concept of the digital submission form to gather raw, unbiased feedback.

Operational Habit Validation: Analyze whether the proposed digital workflow (specifically the 6-field limit and mobile entry) aligns with the "on-the-ground" behavior of a busy kitchen or market staff during a shift.

CRM Intelligence Logging: Use the CRM button in the Meeting Room to document all field insights and stakeholder profiles. 
 Data Management: Ensure you are managing the prospect list (entering/editing) so the entire team has a "Data Source of Truth" regarding market interest.

Strategic Resource Management: Use the Resources button to upload and link relevant market research, competitive analysis, or social media examples. 
 Benchmark: Ensure the team has access to these references for inspiration.

Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Present your "Field Intelligence" at the NSM. 
 Prioritization: Help the team prioritize form fields and validation logic based on real-world urgency versus technical "nice-to-haves."
Sprint 4 Staggered Look-Ahead: Work with the PM to identify the key stakeholders needed for the Contribution Management module validation, which will be your primary focus next week.

Cards.Name: Tactical Planning & Design Alignment
Cards.Description:
This week, you are the Bridge between the high-level journey and the actual build.

Your mission is to translate the vision from Sprint 1 into a detailed, actionable User Story for the Donor Submission Experience.

Sprint 2 is about removing every technical and logical blocker before we start coding in Sprint 3.

You are the team's Unlocker.

We are focusing entirely on the "Input Foundation" - capturing the needs of diverse donor profiles like restaurant owners, bakery managers, and market vendors.

Success means a donor can seamlessly enter their details, list surplus items, and submit a donation via a mobile-optimized form.

By the end of this week, the team must know exactly what to build, why, and how.

Every decision you document now regarding the "Donor Experience" ensures the developers and designers can execute the build with total focus and zero ambiguity.
ChecklistItems: Meeting Orchestration: Schedule a short Sprint 2 Status Meeting at the start of the week for team alignment. Schedule the Next Sprint Meeting (NSM) for the second half of the week.
Status Sync Recap: Publish a concise summary of the week's setup tasks and any immediate blockers identified during the status meeting to the Team Chat.

Customer Validation: Interview the AI Customer to finalize the required Donor Form fields (limit to max 6) and identify the specific IF/THEN validation rules, such as rules for specific delivery days or item quantity limits.

The User Story Deep-Dive: Locate the existing Donor Submission Form card in the "User Stories" Trello list and fill it out completely: 
 (1) Description Field: Write the formal story ("As a... I want... so that..."), define the In-Scope/Out-of-Scope lists, and include a Data Table listing every Field Name, Field Type (Text, Number, etc.), and whether it is Required (Yes/No). 
 (2) Acceptance Criteria Checklist: Populate the pre-made, empty checklist on the card with specific "Done" conditions, for example, "Success message appears" or "Mandatory field errors trigger correctly".

Design Alignment: Sync with the UI/UX Designer to review the Donation Form Design and success screens. Ensure the final design, including the visual language, is saved in the Squad Room before the sprint ends.

Technical Requirements Sync: Meet with the Backend Developer once your User Story is drafted. Help them identify the specific Tables and Fields needed to support the Donor Form logic based on your Data Specification.
Technical Readiness Audit: Verify the development environment. Confirm the BE has the Schema ready and has generated Mock Data, and that the FE has built the UI Atoms and Navigation Hallways.

Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Lead the planning session. 
 Agenda: 
 (1) Present the User Story, Data Specs, and AC 
 (2) Facilitate T-shirt sizing (S/M/L) 
 (3) Confirm developer understanding 
 (4) Assign owners
Sprint 3 Launch Sync: Publish a consolidated summary of the build goals and a clear list of prioritized action items in the Team Chat to formally trigger the start of the execution phase.

Cards.Name: Flow Visualization & Interactive Prototyping
Cards.Description:
This week, you translate research into reality.

Your mission is to move from the mood boards and personas of Sprint 1 directly into a high-fidelity Visual Language that establishes trust and clarity.

You are the Guardian of the Brand. By the end of this week, the developers should have a "Design Source of Truth" including the final color palette, typography, and polished UI layouts for the Donor Submission Form.

Your goal is to ensure the interface doesn't just work, it feels professional, reliable, and accessible from the very first screen.
ChecklistItems: Logo & Visual Language: Interview the AI Customer regarding the product name, logo guidelines, and overall visual direction. Design the product logo and establish the visual language in Figma (e.g., Color Palette and Typography).
Customer Requirement Sync: Interview the AI Customer to identify specific user needs for the Donor Submission Form and verify any functional expectations.

PM Requirement Sync: Meet with the PM as soon as the Donor Submission Form user story is drafted. Review the required data fields and validation logic to ensure your design supports all business and technical constraints.
Donation Form Design: Create a mobile-first design of the donation form in Figma. Focus on layout hierarchy and ease of use on smaller screens to ensure a smooth donor experience.

States & Notifications: Design success and error states for all form fields and add them to the Figma design system. Create the corresponding success and error notifications for the post-submission flow.

Technical Handshake: Sync with the Frontend Developer to review your designs. Ensure general synchronization regarding technical feasibility and confirm that your UI components align with the project's component library.
Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Participate in the NSM to present the final design to the team. This will serve as the "North Star" for the upcoming development in Sprint 3.
Sprint 4 Look-Ahead: Briefly coordinate with the Product Manager to identify the core requirements for the Food Donation Management module. This will be your research focus starting next week.

Cards.Name: Feature Logic & API Execution
Cards.Description:
This is it. The Build Week. 
 
 You are no longer just an architect. You are the Engine Specialist. 
 
 Your mission is to build the pipes and the storage that allow a user to submit their details successfully. 
 
 By the end of this sprint, when a user clicks "Submit" on the frontend, your backend must catch that data, verify it against the business rules, and save it permanently to the database. 
 
 You are responsible for ensuring that the data "Input Foundation" is secure, accurate, and ready for the next stage of the project.
ChecklistItems: Sprint 3 Launch: Participate in the Sprint Meeting led by the PM. Review the User Story and Acceptance Criteria to ensure you are 100% aligned on the build goals.
Environment Setup: Create your Sprint 3 branch via the mentor buttons panel to begin the build.
Database Migration: Implement the physical tables in the database based on the schema you finalized in Sprint 2. Ensure all fields (IDs, timestamps, and data types) are correctly configured.
Validation Engine Build: Code the IF/THEN logic as defined in the PM's User Story (e.g., ensuring a quantity isn't zero or a date isn't in the past).

API Endpoint Development: Build the POST endpoint that will receive the form data. Ensure the response body includes all necessary parameters for UI responsiveness (e.g., specific error codes, unique record IDs, or updated state objects).
Unit Testing: Write and execute unit tests for your validation logic and database service. Ensure that "bad data" is rejected with the correct error codes and "good data" is prepared for storage.
Peer Code Review: Share your code with the AI Mentor for a review session to ensure your logic is clean, secure, and follows best practices.

Integration Test (Internal): Use your "Mock Data" from last week to test the endpoint. Confirm that valid data is saved and invalid data triggers the correct error response codes before involving the frontend.

Frontend Handshake: Pair with the Frontend Developer to "wire up" the form. Verify that their UI components are successfully sending the data to your endpoint and receiving the expected response parameters.

Next Module Requirements Sync: Meet with the PM once the Donation Management User Story is drafted to identify the specific tables and fields needed to support the upcoming logic based on the new Data Specification.
Technical Readiness Audit: Prepare the environment for the PM's audit by ensuring the Donation Management schema is ready and comprehensive mock data has been generated for the next phase.

Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Participate in the NSM to plan the development of the Donation Management module. This session serves as the primary planning phase for the upcoming sprint, though it can also be used to demonstrate that the input data is being successfully captured and stored.
Feature Shipping (PR & Merge): Push your final code, create a Pull Request (PR), and merge it into main via the mentor buttons panel to officially complete the Input Foundation.

Cards.Name: UI Logic & API Integration
Cards.Description:
This is the Build Week. 
 
 You are no longer just building components. You are building the Experience. 
 
 Your mission is to take the "Atoms" from Sprint 2 and wire them into a fully functional, responsive form that handles user input and server feedback. 
 
 By the end of this sprint, a user should be able to fill out the form, see real-time validation, and receive a clear success or error message from the server. 
 
 You are responsible for the "Handshake" between the user and the data engine.
ChecklistItems: Sprint 3 Launch: Participate in the Sprint Meeting led by the PM. Review the User Story and Acceptance Criteria to ensure the UI behavior matches the business requirements.
Environment Setup: Create your Sprint 3 branch via the mentor buttons panel to begin the build.

Temporary Access Point: Add a "Donor Form" button to the index.html file "frontend-buttons" panel. This ensures the form is accessible to all team members via the "Live Project" link during development. Note that a professional Landing Page will be properly designed toward the end of the project (Sprint 7).
Form Logic Implementation: Transform your static components into a dynamic form. Implement the local state management to capture user input for all fields defined in the PM's Data Table.
Client-Side Validation: Code the immediate UI feedback logic (e.g., preventing empty submissions, checking number ranges, or ensuring correct date formats) before the data even hits the server.
API Integration (The Wiring): Connect your form to the Backend's POST endpoint. Implement the fetch or axios calls to send the user data to the server.

Responsive Feedback & Error Handling: Use the server's response parameters to trigger UI changes. Build the logic for loading states, specific field error messages, and the redirect to the Success "Hallway."
Peer Code Review: Share your code with the AI Mentor for a review session to ensure your state management is efficient and your UI logic is robust.
Integration Test (Internal): Run a full end-to-end test of the flow. Confirm that the form correctly handles both a "Happy Path" (success) and "Edge Cases" (validation errors).
Backend Handshake: Pair with the Backend Developer to verify that your "Request" body perfectly matches their endpoint's expectations and that you are correctly parsing their "Response" parameters.

Next Module Requirements Sync: Meet with the PM once the Donation Management User Story is drafted to identify any unique UI requirements, table structures, or navigation hallways needed for the next phase.
Technical Readiness Audit: Prepare the environment for the PM's audit by ensuring the Donation Management UI atoms and navigation hallways are built and ready for the Sprint 4 integration.

Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Participate in the NSM to plan the development of the Donation Management module. This session serves as the primary planning phase for the upcoming sprint, though it can also be used to demonstrate the live input flow and successful navigation to the confirmation screen.
Feature Shipping (PR & Merge): Push your final code, create a Pull Request (PR), and merge it into main via the mentor buttons panel to officially complete the Input Experience.

Cards.Name: Strategic Pulse and Management Validation
Cards.Description:
This week, you serve as the project's Strategic Pulse.

Your mission is to maintain a dual-stream focus: Internal Alignment to deeply understand the next phase of the product's technical evolution, and External Validation to ground the upcoming Donation Management module in market reality.

You are responsible for ensuring that the management experience is not just a technical dashboard, but a tool that fits the high-speed operational needs of those overseeing food distribution.

You will bridge the gap between the development team's potential constraints and the actual operational habits of staff, volunteers, and logistics coordinators.
ChecklistItems: Customer Sync: At the start of the week, consult with the AI Customer to identify "unknowns" regarding the Donation Management module. Clarify management details that will affect the build, such as how staff prefer to navigate order lists and detailed item views.

Internal Product Alignment: Once the PM and UI/UX Designer have drafted the initial User Story and design for the Donation Management module, meet to conduct a deep-dive. Ensure you can articulate exactly how the management logic and status transitions are intended to work.

Field Stakeholder Outreach: Conduct targeted interviews with 2-3 potential "Managers" or "Coordinators" (e.g., non-profit staff, food bank supervisors, or logistics leads). Present the high-level concept of the management dashboard to gather raw, unbiased feedback.

Operational Habit Validation: Analyze whether the proposed digital management workflow (specifically the data filtering and record processing) aligns with the "on-the-ground" behavior of a supervisor managing incoming donations.

CRM Intelligence Logging: Use the CRM button in the Meeting Room to document all management-related insights and stakeholder profiles. Ensure you are managing the prospect list so the team has a "Data Source of Truth" regarding the needs of internal users.

Strategic Resource Management: Use the Resources button to upload and link relevant market research regarding logistics dashboards or management software. Ensure the team has access to these benchmarks for inspiration.

Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Present your "Field Intelligence" at the NSM. Help the team prioritize dashboard features and business logic based on real-world operational urgency versus technical "nice-to-haves."
Sprint 5 Staggered Look-Ahead: Work with the PM to identify the key stakeholders needed for the validation of the Food Collection Routing module, which will be your primary focus next week.

Cards.Name: Tactical Blueprint and User Story
Cards.Description:
This week, you are the Bridge between the current build and the next phase of the project.

Your mission is to translate the vision from the global roadmap into a detailed, actionable User Story for the Donation Management module.

Sprint 3 is about removing every technical and logical blocker before we start coding in Sprint 4.

You are the team's Unlocker.

We are shifting focus from data entry to data management, exploring how internal users will interact with, process, and oversee the information flowing into the system.

Success means defining a clear, actionable workflow that transforms raw data into organized, manageable outcomes.

By the end of this week, the team must know exactly what to build for the next module, why, and how.

Every decision you document now regarding the Management Experience ensures the developers and designers can execute the build with total focus and zero ambiguity.
ChecklistItems: Meeting Orchestration: Schedule a short Sprint 3 Status Meeting at the start of the week for team alignment. Schedule the Next Sprint Meeting (NSM) for the second half of the week.
Status Sync Recap: Publish a concise summary of the week's setup tasks and any immediate blockers identified during the status meeting to the Team Chat.
Customer Validation: Interview the AI Customer to finalize the required management views (Order List vs. Detailed View) and identify the specific business logic for the Approve/Reject flow.

The User Story Deep-Dive: Locate the existing Donation Management card in the "User Stories" Trello list and fill it out completely: 
 (1) Description Field: Write the formal story ("As a... I want... so that..."), define the In-Scope/Out-of-Scope lists, and include a Data Table listing every Field Name (e.g., Status, Line Items, IDs), Data Type, and required User Actions (e.g., Approve, Reject). 
 (2) Acceptance Criteria Checklist: Populate the pre-made, empty checklist on the card with specific "Done" conditions, such as functional data filtering and sorting or validated logic for record status updates.

Design Alignment: Sync with the UI/UX Designer to review the interface designs and detail views. Ensure the final design, including the visual language, is saved in the Squad Room before the sprint ends.

Technical Requirements Sync: Meet with the Backend Developer once your User Story is drafted. Help them identify the specific Tables and Fields needed to support the Donation Management logic based on your Data Specification.

Technical Readiness Audit: Verify the development environment. Confirm the BE has the Schema ready and has generated Mock Data, and that the FE has built the UI Atoms and Navigation Hallways for the dashboard.

Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Lead the planning session. 
 Agenda: 
 (1) Present the User Story, Data Specs, and AC 
 (2) Facilitate T-shirt sizing (S/M/L) 
 (3) Confirm developer understanding of the management logic 
 (4) Assign owners for Sprint 4
Sprint 4 Launch Sync: Publish a consolidated summary of the build goals and a clear list of prioritized action items in the Team Chat to formally trigger the start of the execution phase.

Cards.Name: Detailed Design and Management Experience
Cards.Description:
This week, you expand the visual ecosystem.

Your mission is to move from the core brand elements of Sprint 2 into a high-fidelity dashboard experience for the Donation Management module.

You are the Guardian of the User Experience.

By the end of this week, the developers should have a "Design Source of Truth" including the layouts for the order lists, detail views, and interactive management elements.

Your goal is to ensure that complex data feels organized, clear, and easy to act upon from any device, maintaining the professional trust established by the brand.
ChecklistItems: Customer Requirement Sync: Interview the AI Customer to identify user priorities, management workflows, and specific data points required for the Donation Management module.

PM Requirement Sync: Meet with the PM as soon as the Donation Management user story is drafted. Review the defined acceptance criteria and data hierarchy to ensure your dashboard design aligns with the administrative workflow.

Dashboard & Detail Design: Create the high-fidelity design for the management interface in Figma. Ensure the layout includes both the order header (for high-level tracking) and the line items (for granular contribution details).

Action States & Feedback: Design the interactive elements for the management flow, such as buttons for user actions and visual feedback for status changes. Ensure these states follow the established visual language and color palette.

Technical Handshake: Sync with the Frontend Developer to review your designs for the upcoming module. Ensure general synchronization regarding technical feasibility and confirm that the UI components align with the established design system.
Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Participate in the NSM to present the final Donation Management designs to the team. This will serve as the "North Star" for the upcoming development in Sprint 4.

Sprint 5 Look-Ahead: Briefly coordinate with the Product Manager to identify the core requirements for the Food Collection Routing module. This will be your research and design focus starting next week.

Cards.Name: Management Logic and Data Retrieval
Cards.Description:
This week, you transition from the entry point to the control room.

You are no longer just building pipes for input. You are the Management Engine Specialist.

Your mission is to build the retrieval systems and logic gates that allow staff to oversee, filter, and process incoming contributions.

By the end of this sprint, your backend must be able to serve complex data sets—including both headers and line items—to the frontend and execute status updates based on business actions.

You are responsible for ensuring that the Donation Management foundation is robust, performant, and ready to support the operational workflow.
ChecklistItems: Sprint 4 Launch: Participate in the Sprint Meeting led by the PM. Review the User Story and Acceptance Criteria to ensure you are 100% aligned on the management build goals.
Environment Setup: Create your Sprint 4 branch via the mentor buttons panel to begin the build.
Database Migration/Updates: Implement any structural changes or new fields required for status tracking and management views. Ensure indexes are correctly set for efficient filtering and sorting.

Query Logic Build: Code the retrieval logic to fetch both high-level order headers and granular line items. Ensure the data is structured to support the "drill-down" behavior defined in the PM's User Story.

API Endpoint Development: Build the GET endpoints for the dashboard and detailed views, alongside the PATCH/PUT endpoints to handle Approve/Reject actions. Ensure the response body includes the updated state objects for UI responsiveness.
Unit Testing: Write and execute unit tests for your retrieval logic and Approve/Reject functions. Ensure that unauthorized or invalid state changes are rejected with the correct error codes.
Peer Code Review: Share your code with the AI Mentor for a review session to ensure your logic is clean, secure, and follows best practices.

Integration Test (Internal): Use your "Mock Data" from Sprint 3 to verify the dashboard endpoints. Confirm that the filters return the correct data and that status updates persist in the database before involving the frontend.

Frontend Handshake: Pair with the Frontend Developer to "wire up" the dashboard. Verify that your endpoints are serving the correct JSON structure for the header/line-item views and responding correctly to user actions.

Next Module Requirements Sync: Meet with the PM once the Food Collection Routing User Story is drafted to identify the specific tables and fields needed to support the upcoming logic based on the new Data Specification.
Technical Readiness Audit: Prepare the environment for the PM's audit by ensuring the Food Collection Routing schema is ready and comprehensive mock data has been generated for the next phase.

Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Participate in the NSM to plan the development of the Food Collection Routing module. This session serves as the primary planning phase for the upcoming sprint, though it can also be used to demonstrate the functional dashboard logic and data processing.
Feature Shipping (PR & Merge): Push your final code, create a Pull Request (PR), and merge it into main via the mentor buttons panel to officially complete the Contribution Management module.

Cards.Name: Management UI and Data Interaction
Cards.Description:
This week, you transition from data collection to data orchestration. 
 
 You are no longer just building input forms. You are building the Management Experience. 
 
 Your mission is to take the dashboard atoms and navigation hallways from Sprint 3 and wire them into a fully functional interface for staff to oversee and process incoming contributions. 
 
 By the end of this sprint, a user should be able to navigate a dynamic list of orders, drill down into granular line items, and execute status changes through responsive Approve/Reject actions. 
 
 You are responsible for ensuring that complex data sets are rendered clearly and that every administrative action provides immediate, reliable feedback.
ChecklistItems: Sprint 4 Launch: Participate in the Sprint Meeting led by the PM. Review the User Story and Acceptance Criteria to ensure the dashboard behavior and action logic match the business requirements.
Environment Setup: Create your Sprint 4 branch via the mentor buttons panel to begin the build.

Temporary Access Point: Add a "Management Dashboard" button to the index.html file "frontend-buttons" panel. This ensures the management interface is accessible to all team members via the "Live Project" link during development. As a reminder, the final professional Landing Page will be designed in Sprint 7.

Dashboard Logic Implementation: Transform your static table and list atoms into a dynamic interface. Implement state management to handle the display of order headers and the "drill-down" logic for nested line items.

Filtering & Sorting Engine: Code the client-side logic for data organization. Ensure users can seamlessly filter and sort the donor donation list based on the parameters defined in the PM's User Story.
API Integration (The Wiring): Connect your dashboard to the Backend’s GET endpoints for list/detail views and the PATCH/PUT endpoints for Approve/Reject actions.

Responsive Action Handling: Build the logic for interactive feedback. Ensure that clicking Approve or Reject triggers the correct loading states and updates the UI in real-time based on the server's response.
Peer Code Review: Share your code with the AI Mentor for a review session to ensure your logic is clean, secure, and follows best practices.

Integration Test (Internal): Run a full end-to-end test of the management flow. Confirm that the dashboard correctly handles data rendering, filtering, and the successful execution of status-change actions.

Backend Handshake: Pair with the Backend Developer to verify that your data requirements for the "Header + Line Item" view match their response structure and that action parameters are correctly aligned.

Next Module Requirements Sync: Meet with the PM once the Food Collection Routing User Story is drafted to identify any unique UI requirements, map components, or navigation hallways needed for the next phase.
Technical Readiness Audit: Prepare the environment for the PM’s audit by ensuring the Food Collection Routing UI atoms and navigation hallways are built and ready for the Sprint 5 integration.

Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Participate in the NSM to plan the development of the Food Collection Routing module. This session serves as the primary planning phase for the upcoming sprint, though it can also be used to demonstrate the live management flow, showing the dashboard navigation and the successful processing of a contribution.
Feature Shipping (PR & Merge): Push your final code, create a Pull Request (PR), and merge it into main via the mentor buttons panel to officially complete the Contribution Management experience.

Cards.Name: Strategic Pulse and Logistics Validation
Cards.Description:
This week, you serve as the project's Strategic Pulse. 
 
 Your mission is to maintain a dual-stream focus: Internal Alignment to deeply understand the product's technical evolution into logistics, and External Validation to ground the upcoming Food Collection Routing module in market reality. 
 
 You are responsible for ensuring that the routing experience is not just a technical map, but a functional tool that fits the real-time operational needs of those physically moving the food. 
 
 You will bridge the gap between the development team's potential constraints and the actual operational habits of drivers, dispatchers, and logistics volunteers.
ChecklistItems: [ ] Customer Sync: At the start of the week, consult with the AI Customer to identify "unknowns" regarding the Food Collection Routing module. Clarify logistics details that will affect the build, such as how drivers prioritize stops and what data they need while on the road.

[ ] Internal Product Alignment: Once the PM and UI/UX Designer have drafted the initial User Story and design for the Food Collection Routing module, meet to conduct a deep-dive. Ensure you can articulate exactly how the routing logic and map interactions are intended to work.

[ ] Field Stakeholder Outreach: Conduct targeted interviews with 2–3 potential "Drivers" or "Logistics Leads" (e.g., NPO delivery drivers, volunteer coordinators, or professional couriers). Present the high-level concept of the routing interface to gather raw, unbiased feedback.
[ ] Operational Habit Validation: Analyze whether the proposed digital routing workflow aligns with the "on-the-ground" behavior of a driver managing a multi-stop route during a busy shift.

[ ] CRM Intelligence Logging: Use the CRM button in the Meeting Room to document all logistics-related insights and stakeholder profiles. Ensure you are managing the prospect list so the team has a "Data Source of Truth" regarding the needs of the mobile/field users.

[ ] Strategic Resource Management: Use the Resources button to upload and link relevant market research regarding routing software, map APIs, or logistics benchmarks. Ensure the team has access to these references for inspiration.

[ ] Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Present your "Field Intelligence" at the NSM. Help the team prioritize routing features and map logic based on real-world operational urgency versus technical "nice-to-haves."
[ ] Sprint 6 Staggered Look-Ahead: Work with the PM to identify the key stakeholders needed for the validation of the Reports module, which will be your primary focus next week.

Cards.Name: Tactical Blueprint and User Story
Cards.Description:
This week, you are the Bridge between the current build and the next logistical phase of the project. 
 
 Your mission is to translate the vision from the global roadmap into a detailed, actionable User Story for the Food Collection Routing module. 
 
 Sprint 4 is about removing every technical and logical blocker before we start coding the routing features in Sprint 5. 
 
 You are the team's Unlocker. 
 
 We are shifting focus from internal data management to field operations, exploring how spatial data and service integrations will be used to facilitate physical food recovery. 
 
 Success means defining a clear, actionable workflow that transforms static address data into an optimized, navigable route for the team. 
 
 By the end of this week, the team must know exactly what to build for the next module, why, and how. 
 
 Every decision you document now regarding the Logistics Experience ensures the developers and designers can execute the build with total focus and zero ambiguity.
ChecklistItems: [ ] Meeting Orchestration: Schedule a short Sprint 4 Status Meeting at the start of the week for team alignment. Schedule the Next Sprint Meeting (NSM) for the second half of the week.
[ ] Status Sync Recap: Publish a concise summary of the week's setup tasks and any immediate blockers identified during the status meeting to the Team Chat.

[ ] Customer Validation: Interview the AI Customer to finalize the required routing views (e.g., Route Overview vs. Stop Details) and identify the specific business logic for daily route generation and location-based sequences.

[ ] The User Story Deep-Dive: Locate the existing Food Collection Routing card in the "User Stories" Trello list and fill it out completely: \n (1) Description Field: Write the formal story ("As a driver... I want... so that..."), define the In-Scope/Out-of-Scope lists, and include a Data Table listing every Field Name (e.g., Address, Coordinates, Stop Order), Data Type, and required User Actions. \n (2) Acceptance Criteria Checklist: Populate the pre-made, empty checklist on the card with specific "Done" conditions relevant to the module’s core logic and technical requirements.

[ ] Design Alignment: Sync with the UI/UX Designer to review the interface designs for map integration and mobile routing views. Ensure the final design, including the visual language, is saved in the Squad Room before the sprint ends.

[ ] Technical Requirements Sync: Meet with the Backend Developer once your User Story is drafted. Help them identify the specific Tables and Fields needed to support the Food Collection Routing logic and external service interactions.

[ ] Technical Readiness Audit: Verify the development environment. Confirm the BE has the Schema ready and has generated Mock Data, and that the FE has built the UI Atoms and Navigation Hallways for the map-based views.

[ ] Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Lead the planning session. 
 Agenda: 
 (1) Present the User Story, Data Specs, and AC 
 (2) Facilitate T-shirt sizing (S/M/L) 
 (3) Confirm developer understanding of the routing logic 
 (4) Assign owners for Sprint 5
[ ] Sprint 5 Launch Sync: Publish a consolidated summary of the build goals and a clear list of prioritized action items in the Team Chat to formally trigger the start of the execution phase.

Cards.Name: Detailed Design and Logistics Experience
Cards.Description:
This week, you translate the logistical roadmap into a high-utility interface for the field. 
 
 Your mission is to design the mobile-first "Driver Command Center" for the Food Collection Routing module. 
 
 You are the Guardian of the User Experience. 
 
 By the end of this week, the developers should have a "Design Source of Truth" that defines how a driver interacts with their daily route, from navigational cues to stop management. 
 
 Your goal is to ensure that the interface is glanceable and functional, providing the driver with the essential information needed to navigate and collect donations without unnecessary complexity.
ChecklistItems: Customer Requirement Sync: Interview the AI Customer to identify driver priorities, essential field data points, and the preferred workflow for navigating between collection stops.

PM Requirement Sync: Meet with the PM as soon as the Food Collection Routing user story is drafted. Review the defined stops, status logic, and navigational requirements to ensure your mobile framework is aligned with the operational plan.

Driver Interface Design: Create the high-level mobile design for the routing experience in Figma. Design Focus: Establish the visual framework for navigating daily routes, including map placeholders or visual links and summaries of scheduled collection points.

Field Action States: Design the interactive elements for the driver’s workflow, such as buttons for status updates and navigational triggers. Ensure these actions provide immediate visual confirmation using the established color palette.

Technical Handshake: Sync with the Frontend Developer to review your designs. 
 Synchronization: Confirm the technical approach for integrating map placeholders or external navigational links. 
 Feasibility: Ensure the UI components are optimized for mobile performance and accessibility.

Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Participate in the NSM to present the final Food Collection Routing designs to the team. 
 Agenda: 
 (1) Present the High-Level Driver Interface and Routing layouts 
 (2) Demonstrate the core navigational flow 
 (3) Confirm design handoff for Sprint 5 execution
Sprint 6 Look-Ahead: Briefly coordinate with the Product Manager to identify the core requirements for the Reporting and Analytics module. This will be your research focus starting next week.

Cards.Name: Logistics Engine and Routing Logic
Cards.Description:
This week, the backend goes mobile. 
 
 You are no longer just an administrator of data; you are the Logistics Engine Specialist. 
 
 Your mission is to build the computational logic and external service handshakes that transform static addresses into optimized collection routes. 
 
 By the end of this sprint, your backend must be able to process location data, interface with mapping services, and serve a sequenced daily route to the driver’s interface. 
 
 You are responsible for ensuring that the Food Collection Routing foundation is reliable and provides the necessary endpoints for real-time status updates in the field.
ChecklistItems: Sprint 5 Launch: Participate in the Sprint Meeting led by the PM. Review the User Story and Acceptance Criteria to ensure you are 100% aligned on the logistics build goals.
Environment Setup: Create your Sprint 5 branch via the mentor buttons panel to begin the build.

Database Migration/Updates: Implement any structural changes or new fields required for logistics tracking and routing views. Ensure necessary performance optimizations are implemented for efficient data retrieval.

Routing Logic Build: Conduct a technical deep-dive into the Google Maps API documentation to identify critical parameters for route optimization and traffic considerations. Code the logic to transform donor locations into sequenced daily routes, consulting with the AI Mentor to ensure the external service integration is secure and performant.
API Endpoint Development: Build the GET endpoints for the daily route summary and individual stop details, alongside the PATCH/PUT endpoints to handle status transitions (e.g., Arrived, Collected).
Unit Testing: Write and execute unit tests for your routing logic and status update functions. Ensure that coordinate processing and external service handshakes are handled gracefully.
Peer Code Review: Share your code with the AI Mentor for a review session to ensure your logic is clean, secure, and follows best practices.

Integration Test (Internal): Use your "Mock Data" from Sprint 4 to verify the routing endpoints. Confirm that the sequence logic returns the expected order of stops and that status updates persist correctly.

Frontend Handshake: Pair with the Frontend Developer to "wire up" the driver interface. Verify that your endpoints are serving the correct data structure for map placeholders and that navigational triggers are functional.

Next Module Requirements Sync: Meet with the PM once the Reports User Story is drafted to identify the specific tables and fields needed to support the upcoming logic based on the new Data Specification.
Technical Readiness Audit: Prepare the environment for the PM's audit by ensuring the Reporting and Analytics schema is ready and comprehensive mock data has been generated for the next phase.

Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Participate in the NSM to plan the development of the Reporting and Analytics module. This session serves as the primary planning phase for the upcoming sprint, though it can also be used to demonstrate the functional routing logic.
Feature Shipping (PR & Merge): Push your final code, create a Pull Request (PR), and merge it into main via the mentor buttons panel to officially complete the Food Collection Routing module.

Cards.Name: Logistics and Routing Interface
Cards.Description:
This week, the interface hits the road. 
 
 Your mission is to build the mobile-first Driver Command Center for the Food Collection Routing module. 
 
 You are no longer building for a desktop dashboard; you are building for a driver in the field who needs high glanceability and one-handed interaction. 
 
 By the end of this sprint, your interface must be able to render a sequenced list of stops, integrate map placeholders or visual links, and handle real-time status updates from the field. 
 
 You are responsible for ensuring that the logistical flow is intuitive, responsive, and robust enough to handle the physical movement of food recovery.
ChecklistItems: Sprint 5 Launch: Participate in the Sprint Meeting led by the PM. Review the User Story and Acceptance Criteria to ensure the mobile routing behavior matches the driver's workflow.
Environment Setup: Create your Sprint 5 branch via the mentor buttons panel to begin the build.

Temporary Access Point: Add a "Driver Routing" button to the index.html file "frontend-buttons" panel. This ensures the routing interface is accessible to all team members via the "Live Project" link during development. As a reminder, the final professional Landing Page will be designed in Sprint 7.
Mobile Route Implementation: Transform your routing atoms into a dynamic, mobile-optimized list. Implement state management to handle the display of the daily stop sequence.

Map & Navigation Integration: Implement the map placeholders or visual links as defined in the UI/UX design. Build the logic to receive and render navigation URLs dynamically from the Backend API once the route is generated.
API Integration (The Wiring): Connect your interface to the Backend’s GET endpoints for route data and the PATCH/PUT endpoints for status updates (e.g., "Arrived," "Collected").
Field Action Handling: Build the logic for mobile status transitions. Ensure that the UI provides clear, immediate feedback when a stop is marked as complete or if an issue is reported.
Peer Code Review: Share your code with the AI Mentor for a review session to ensure your mobile responsiveness and state transitions are efficient.
Integration Test (Internal): Run a full end-to-end test of the routing flow on a mobile viewport. Confirm that the sequence renders correctly and that status changes persist through the API.
Backend Handshake: Pair with the Backend Developer to verify that the coordinate data, stop sequencing, and dynamic navigation links in the JSON structure match your interface requirements.
Next Module Requirements Sync: Meet with the PM once the Reports User Story is drafted to identify any unique UI requirements, data visualization needs, or navigation hallways for the next phase.
Technical Readiness Audit: Prepare the environment for the PM’s audit by ensuring the Reporting and Analytics UI atoms and navigation hallways are built and ready for the Sprint 6 integration.

Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Participate in the NSM to plan the development of the Reporting and Analytics module. This session serves as the primary planning phase for the upcoming sprint, though it can also be used to demonstrate the live routing flow and mobile field interactions.
Feature Shipping (PR & Merge): Push your final code, create a Pull Request (PR), and merge it into main via the mentor buttons panel to officially complete the Food Collection Routing experience.

Cards.Name: Strategic Pulse and Analytics Validation
Cards.Description:
This week, you transition from the physical logistics of the road to the strategic intelligence of the data room.

Your mission is to maintain a dual-stream focus: Internal Alignment to deeply understand how we aggregate the data collected from the previous modules, and External Validation to ensure the Reporting and Analytics module provides actual, actionable value to NPO managers.

You are responsible for ensuring that the reporting experience is not just a collection of tables, but a powerful tool for tracking performance, identifying trends, and proving impact.

You will bridge the gap between technical data aggregation and the real-world decision-making habits of NPO directors, donor managers, and community leaders.
ChecklistItems: Customer Sync: At the start of the week, consult with the AI Customer to identify "unknowns" regarding the Reporting and Analytics module. Clarify which metrics (e.g., Weekly Champions vs. Historical Logs) carry the most weight for donor retention and operational strategy.

Internal Product Alignment: Once the PM and UI/UX Designer have drafted the initial User Story and design for the Reporting and Analytics module, meet to conduct a deep-dive. Ensure you can articulate the strategic utility of the reporting architecture, specifically how the interplay between filtering parameters and donor-level data generates high-impact business intelligence for the organization.

Strategic Stakeholder Outreach: Conduct targeted interviews with 2–3 potential NPO Managers or Impact Coordinators. Present the high-level concept of the "Weekly Champions" and "Daily Contributions" views to determine if these analytics align with their reporting requirements.

Data-Driven Habit Validation: Analyze whether the proposed reporting format (tabular vs. graphical) fits the existing "on-the-ground" behavior of managers who currently use spreadsheets or manual logs for data analysis.

CRM Intelligence Logging: Use the CRM button in the Meeting Room to document all analytics-related insights and stakeholder profiles. Ensure you are managing the prospect list so the team has a "Data Source of Truth" regarding the reporting needs of organizational leadership.

Strategic Resource Management: Use the Resources button to upload and link relevant market research regarding data visualization benchmarks, NPO impact metrics, or donor recognition trends. Ensure the team has access to these references for inspiration.
Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Present your "Analytics Intelligence" at the NSM. Help the team prioritize report types and visualization styles based on real-world urgency versus technical complexity.
Sprint 7 Staggered Look-Ahead: Work with the PM to identify the key stakeholders needed for the validation of the next phase of the project roadmap, which will be your primary focus next week.

Cards.Name: Tactical Blueprint and User Story
Cards.Description:
This week, you are the Bridge between operational logistics and strategic intelligence.

Your mission is to translate the vision from the global roadmap into a detailed, actionable User Story for the Reporting and Analytics module.

Sprint 5 is about removing every technical and logical blocker before we start coding the analytics and reporting features in Sprint 6.

You are the team's Unlocker.

We are shifting focus from field operations (routing) to data visualization and impact assessment, exploring how complex queries and data aggregation will provide value to NPO managers.

Success means defining a clear, actionable workflow that transforms raw database records into insightful reports, daily summaries, and donor recognition metrics.

By the end of this week, the team must know exactly what to build for the next module, why, and how.

Every decision you document now regarding the Analytics Experience ensures the developers and designers can execute the build with total focus and zero ambiguity.
ChecklistItems: Meeting Orchestration: Schedule a short Sprint 5 Status Meeting at the start of the week for team alignment. Schedule the Next Sprint Meeting (NSM) for the second half of the week.
Status Sync Recap: Publish a concise summary of the week's setup tasks and any immediate blockers identified during the status meeting to the Team Chat.

Customer Validation: Interview the AI Customer to finalize the required reporting views (e.g., Daily Contributions vs. Weekly Champions) and identify the specific business logic for data aggregation and date-range filtering.

The User Story Deep-Dive: Locate the existing Reporting and Analytics card in the "User Stories" Trello list and fill it out completely: 
 (1) Description Field: Write the formal story ("As an NPO Manager... I want... so that..."), define the In-Scope/Out-of-Scope lists, and include a Data Table listing every Report Type, required Parameters (e.g., Date Range, Donor ID), and intended Output (e.g., Table, Chart, CSV). 
 (2) Acceptance Criteria Checklist: Populate the pre-made, empty checklist on the card with specific "Done" conditions, such as "Charts render correctly for empty data sets" or "Weekly Champion logic correctly identifies the top donor by volume."

Design Alignment: Sync with the UI/UX Designer to review the interface designs for data visualization and report tables. Ensure the final design, including the visual language for charts, is saved in the Squad Room before the sprint ends.

Technical Requirements Sync: Meet with the Backend Developer once your User Story is drafted. Help them identify the specific Aggregation Queries and Data Joins needed to support the Reporting and Analytics logic and performance requirements.

Technical Readiness Audit: Verify the development environment. Confirm the BE has the aggregation logic/mock data ready, and that the FE has built the UI Atoms (charts, tables) and Navigation Hallways for the reporting module.

Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Lead the planning session. 
 Agenda: 
 (1) Present the User Story, Data Specs, and AC 
 (2) Facilitate T-shirt sizing (S/M/L) 
 (3) Confirm developer understanding of the reporting and aggregation logic 
 (4) Assign owners for Sprint 6
Sprint 6 Launch Sync: Publish a consolidated summary of the build goals and a clear list of prioritized action items in the Team Chat to formally trigger the start of the execution phase.

Cards.Name: Detailed Design and Analytics Experience
Cards.Description:
This week, you transition from the physical logistics of the road to the strategic intelligence of the data room.

Your mission is to design the "Manager’s Analytics Hub" for the Reporting and Analytics module.

You are the Guardian of the User Experience.

By the end of this week, the developers should have a "Design Source of Truth" that defines how an NPO manager visualizes impact, from high-level "Weekly Champion" charts to granular historical logs.

Your goal is to ensure that complex data feels organized and insightful, transforming raw numbers into a clear narrative of community impact without overwhelming the user.
ChecklistItems: Customer Requirement Sync: Interview the AI Customer to identify reporting priorities, key performance indicators (KPIs), and the preferred visualization styles for donor recognition and historical tracking.

PM Requirement Sync: Meet with the PM as soon as the Reporting and Analytics user story is drafted. Review the specific data aggregates, filter logic, and KPI definitions to ensure your layouts accurately reflect the system's reporting capabilities.

Analytics Hub Design: Create the high-fidelity design for the Reporting and Analytics interface in Figma. 
 (1) Data Visualization: Design the framework for graphical reports, such as bar charts for daily contributions and spotlight cards for "Weekly Champions." 
 (2) Tabular Views: Design the high-density layouts for historical order logs, ensuring clear hierarchy and scannable rows. 
 (3) Parameter Controls: Design the UI for custom filters, including date-range pickers and donor-specific search bars.

Interaction & Data States: Design the interactive elements for the reporting flow, such as chart hover states, filter feedback, and export triggers. Ensure these states maintain professional trust through the established visual language and color palette.

Technical Handshake: Sync with the Frontend and Backend Developers to review your designs. 
 Synchronization: Confirm the technical approach for rendering charts and the JSON structure required for high-density tables. 
 Feasibility: Ensure data visualization components are responsive and performant across different device types.

Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Participate in the NSM to present the final Reports module designs to the team. 
 Agenda: 
 (1) Present the Analytics Hub and Report layouts 
 (2) Demonstrate the filtering and visualization flow 
 (3) Confirm design handoff for Sprint 6 execution
Sprint 7 Look-Ahead: Briefly coordinate with the Product Manager to identify the core requirements for the Application Landing Page. This will be your primary design and research focus for Sprint 7.

Cards.Name: Reports and Data Aggregation
Cards.Description:
This week, you transition from the engine room to the observation deck. 
 
 You are now the Data Alchemist. Your mission is to take the raw records captured in previous modules and transform them into structured, high-value business intelligence. 
 
 By the end of this sprint, your backend must be able to perform complex data aggregations and serve the analytical datasets required to power the reporting interface. 
 
 You are responsible for ensuring the reporting engine is performant and that the aggregated data is accurate, consistent, and ready for visualization.
ChecklistItems: Sprint 6 Launch: Participate in the Sprint Meeting led by the PM. Review the User Story and Acceptance Criteria to ensure you are 100% aligned on the reporting and analytics goals.
Environment Setup: Create your Sprint 6 branch via the mentor buttons panel to begin the build.
Database Optimization: Review your existing schema to ensure that performance optimizations are implemented for complex aggregation queries and multi-field filtering.
Aggregation Logic Build: Code the server-side logic to aggregate data for the specific report types defined by the PM. Focus on transforming raw database records into summarized, actionable insights.
API Endpoint Development: Build the GET endpoints for the analytics hub. Implement robust query parameter handling to support dynamic filtering and multi-dimensional data retrieval.
Unit Testing: Write and execute unit tests for your aggregation functions. Ensure that calculations remain accurate and performant across varying dataset sizes.
Peer Code Review: Share your code with the AI Mentor for a review session to ensure your aggregation logic is efficient and follows best practices for data processing.

Integration Test (Internal): Use your "Mock Data" from Sprint 5 to verify the reporting endpoints. Confirm that the JSON response structure perfectly matches the requirements for the frontend components.

Frontend Handshake: Pair with the Frontend Developer to "wire up" the Analytics Hub. Verify that your endpoints respond correctly to interface filters and serve data in a format optimized for rendering.

Next Module Requirements Sync: Meet with the PM once the Food Quality AI Validator add-on service requirements are drafted to identify the specific tables, data types, and storage needs for the upcoming AI logic.

Technical Readiness Audit: Prepare the environment for the PM's audit by ensuring the schema for the Food Quality AI Validator service is ready and that you have a plan for handling the necessary data inputs for the next phase.

Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Participate in the NSM to plan the development of the Food Quality AI Validator add-on. This session serves as the primary planning phase for the upcoming sprint, though it can also be used to demonstrate the functional reporting and aggregation logic.
Feature Shipping (PR & Merge): Push your final code, create a Pull Request (PR), and merge it into main via the mentor buttons panel to officially complete the Reports module.

Cards.Name: Analytics Hub and Data Visualization
Cards.Description:
This week, you transition from operational field tools to the strategic oversight of the platform. 
 
 Your mission is to build the Analytics Hub for the Reports module. 
 
 You are no longer just capturing or managing data; you are responsible for making it meaningful. By the end of this sprint, your interface must be able to render high-density tables and dynamic charts that provide a clear narrative of the platform's impact. 
 
 You are responsible for ensuring that complex datasets are presented with high glanceability and that the filtering logic is seamless, allowing managers to extract the specific insights they need to drive the organization forward.
ChecklistItems: Sprint 6 Launch: Participate in the Sprint Meeting led by the PM. Review the User Story and Acceptance Criteria to ensure the Reporting and Analytics interface and data visualization logic match the business requirements.
Environment Setup: Create your Sprint 6 branch via the mentor buttons panel to begin the build.

Temporary Access Point: Add an "Analytics Hub" button to the index.html file "frontend-buttons" panel. This ensures the Reporting and Analytics interface is accessible to all team members via the "Live Project" link during development. As a reminder, the final professional Landing Page will be the focus of Sprint 7.

Analytics UI Implementation: Transform your reporting atoms into a dynamic interface. Implement the layouts for data visualization widgets (charts/graphs) and high-density tables as defined in the UI/UX designs.

Filtering & Parameter Logic: Code the client-side logic to handle dynamic report parameters. Ensure the interface correctly updates when users apply filters such as dates, specific categories, or donor profiles.

API Integration (The Wiring): Connect your Analytics Hub to the Backend’s GET endpoints for aggregated datasets. Implement the logic to parse complex JSON structures and map them to your chart and table components.

Interaction & Feedback Handling: Build the logic for interactive data states. Ensure the UI provides clear feedback during data fetching (loading states) and handles empty result sets or filtering errors gracefully.
Peer Code Review: Share your code with the AI Mentor for a review session to ensure your data mapping logic is efficient and your chart components are performant.

Integration Test (Internal): Run a full end-to-end test of the reporting flow. Confirm that the interface correctly renders aggregated data and that all filters trigger the expected updates from the API.
Backend Handshake: Pair with the Backend Developer to verify that the aggregated data format served by their endpoints perfectly matches the expectations of your visualization components.

Next Module Requirements Sync: Meet with the PM once the Application Landing Page requirements are drafted to identify the specific visual assets, branding elements, and navigation links needed for the final phase.
Technical Readiness Audit: Prepare the environment for the PM’s audit by ensuring the structural "Atoms" for the Landing Page (hero sections, feature cards) are ready for the Sprint 7 build.

Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Participate in the NSM to plan the development of the Application Landing Page. This session serves as the primary planning phase for the final project sprint, though it can also be used to demonstrate the functional Analytics Hub.
Feature Shipping (PR & Merge): Push your final code, create a Pull Request (PR), and merge it into main via the mentor buttons panel to officially complete the Reports experience.

Cards.Name: Strategic Logic and Impact-ROI Modeling
Cards.Description:
This week, you are transitioning the project from a "functional application" to a "measurable socio-economic solution." 
 
 Your mission is to develop a comprehensive Logic Model that maps the causal link between our technological inputs and the tangible social and economic value we create. 
 
 You are the team's Value Architect. 
 
 Success means establishing a quantitative framework that proves how the platform optimizes resources. By the end of this week, you will define the "Value Equation" to serve as the core data for investor pitches. All finalized documentation must be saved under the Resources section in the Squad Room.
ChecklistItems: [ ] Logic Model Framework (Inputs & Outputs): Define the resources invested and map them to the platform’s technical deliverables (e.g., reporting tools, algorithms), ensuring the Strategic Logic Model Document is finalized and saved to the Resources folder.

[ ] Impact Analysis (Outcomes): Identify specific social and environmental changes, such as reduced food insecurity and lower greenhouse gas emissions, to provide a clear narrative of the platform's community influence.

[ ] Economic ROI Modeling (Business Stakeholders): Analyze financial benefits for participating businesses, including tax incentives, operational savings, and brand equity, and save the finalized ROI & Business Value Analysis to the Resources section.
[ ] Municipal Value Proposition: Calculate the "City Savings" model regarding waste disposal and welfare burdens to support long-term partnership sustainability.
[ ] The "Value Equation" Synthesis: Formulate a high-impact KPI (e.g., 3.5x ROI) to demonstrate efficiency and document the Executive Summary: The Value Equation in the Resources section.
[ ] Pitch Material Integration: Translate the Logic Model and ROI data into high-impact slides, ensuring the Updated Investor Pitch Deck is saved under Resources for upcoming presentations.
[ ] Stakeholder Validation: Meet with the AI Customer to verify that the ROI assumptions align with actual operational costs and budget categories.
[ ] Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Present the completed Logic Model and ROI data to the team to align the marketing narrative with the new quantitative data.

Cards.Name: Tactical Blueprint and Risk-Aware Planning
Cards.Description:
This week, you are managing the transition toward a public-facing platform and an automated risk-assessment layer.

Your mission is to define the requirements for two key areas: the Application Landing Page and the Food Quality Validator AI Agent add-on.

Sprint 6 is about resolving logical and technical dependencies before the final execution in Sprint 7.

You are the team's Unlocker.

Success means establishing a clear structure for the landing page that effectively communicates the platform's purpose and a functional logic for the AI agent that provides an automated secondary oversight for the management flow.

By the end of this week, the team must have the data specifications and user flows necessary to implement these features without technical ambiguity.
ChecklistItems: Meeting Orchestration: Schedule a short Sprint 6 Status Meeting at the start of the week for team alignment. Schedule the Next Sprint Meeting (NSM) for the second half of the week.
